I think the solver options could be handled in a better way. A suggestion:

1. pop all the command line options from the `solver_options` dict with defaults. In the form `parallel = solver_options.pop(parallel, False)`. All of these are added to the command line
2. Set a defaults dict for the `option_file_options` which include the options which you have in line 670-674. 
3. Update the `option_file_options` dict with the remaining `solver_options`, using `option_file_options.update(solver_options)` 
4. Convert the `option_file_options` to text form and write it to the file. 

In this way, one can easily access command line options and option-file options. What do you think?

I also found a few stray objects like `TargetCircle` that fall into a similar category of not having actual colliders, and are effectively made up entirely of trigger colliders. There are also things like houseplants which have their leaves set up to only be triggers so that objects can pass through them, but they still show up on the visibility pass since that checks against both trigger and nontrigger colliders. I think it would be safest to make it so triggers are still included in bounds generation, otherwise many of these sorts of objects will be missed or end up generating incorrect box volumes.

I'm OK with this, but there is a risk here. When you ask the HuggingFace tokenizer to make tensors, it also wants to add padding. We can't have padding in `TransformerTextField`.

It's really annoying that HF tokenizers are basically all-inclusive preprocessing machines. They tokenize, they index, they batch, they concatenate, all in one. If you want to do only one of these things, tough luck.

So this isn't the right _fix_ here. 

See the `tox` file: https://github.com/jazzband/django-silk/blob/master/tox.ini

`tox` lets you run against various versions of different dependencies, and check they're all working. See https://tox.wiki/en/latest/

You'll see that the tests are already running against all supported versions of Django. 4.0 was added in b684b8ccf97a5553a1312ecbf588fc7523121a94.

In fact 3.1 is now EOL — end-of-life — so you can make a PR dropping testing against that.

Could you change the following:

* replace the line where there used to be
   ```
   com_i = n.df(c).query('committable').index.difference(ext_i)
   ```
   with 
   ```
    if "committable" in n.df(c):
        com_i = n.df(c).query('committable').index.difference(ext_i)
    else:
        com_i = []
    ```
* undo the `if committable` case differentiation
* apply the other proposed changes

I noticed that LLVM/clang can emit multiple sections with the same name in order to save space in the resulting binary when -ffunction-sections is used. The current code also doesn't handle this case, so using get_section_by_name() on such an object file will always return the last section with the non-unique name. See the following example:

```
$ cat two_f.c
void a(){}
void b(){}

$ clang-9 -ffunction-sections -fno-unique-section-names -c -o two_f.o two_f.c

$ readelf -S two_f.o
There are 11 section headers, starting at offset 0x208:

Section Headers:
  [Nr] Name              Type             Address           Offset
       Size              EntSize          Flags  Link  Info  Align
  [ 0]                   NULL             0000000000000000  00000000
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000           0     0     0
  [ 1] .strtab           STRTAB           0000000000000000  000001b0
       0000000000000057  0000000000000000           0     0     1

Hi. I went through the changes and everything looks good to me!

> This is awesome, really cool work!

Thanks! Glad to finally be able to contribute!

> For raw output, I agree with the decision to simply print the whole BUILD file. Way too tricky to otherwise try to identify which part is applicable. And we hope/expect BUILD files to be fairly small thanks to dep inference.

I played (a very small bit) with some inspection tools to find the applicable part, but it looked complicated. It could be an issue in its own right if we wanted to pursue it more.
 
> However, I could see it getting confusing when you specify multiple targets/files, and mapping what belongs to what. Thoughts on detecting the case of >1 "spec", and logging a warning to consider using the JSON format? We do that with `./pants py-constraints`:

I don't think a warning is necessary if we make JSON the default output, but I'll defer to your judgement on this.

> The JSON output is excellent: it shows the user what Pants's view of a particular file/target actually is. That transparency is great for debugging. We might want to make JSON the default?
> 
> I'd recommend two tweaks:
> 
>     1. Rather than `alias`, call it `target_type`, or something similar. "Alias" is an implementation detail.
> 
>     2. I think we want to render default values? That way, we show a complete view of what Pants is doing. It also allows for safer JSON queries, as you're guaranteed the key will exist. If it gets too chatty, we could consider an option like `--ignore-defaults`.
> 

I agree with these suggestions.

- [x] Make JSON the default output style
- [x] Rename `alias` in output to `target_type`
- [ ] Show default values by default
- [ ] Add `--exclude-defaults` flag (optional)

> In #4861, we mentioned adding a table format in a followup. I'm not sure that would really be necessary - the mix of Raw and JSON is very helpful already.

Agreed. It's human-readable enough, and machine readable with things like `jq`

> In #4861, I pondered
> 
> > Should [we] evaluate the sources and dependencies fields, or show the raw values?
> 
> After seeing this PR, I think no, we should not. `./pants dependencies` already exists for deps, and `./pants filedeps` for sources. It's redundant imo, and also non-trivial, to include those here, including a performance hit.

Agreed, and if you really want that, `jq` + `xargs` are your friends:

```
./pants peek ... | jq -r ... | xargs ./pants filedeps
```

Although I admit that trying to come up with a good example `jq` query made me realize that it doesn't seem to play well with arbitrary keys like we're using, so perhaps making a list of objects is a better way forward. Any opinion on this, @Eric-Arellano ?

looks great overall! just a couple of questions/nits

one broader questions i want to clarify is what assumptions we are making when accessing the payload of the request object.

if the request is a form request and contains a form-encoded body, the following calls will return
```python
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads from the stream, caches the body, and returns it
request.form                   # parses the form data from the cache and returns the form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # returns the cached body (will contain the original form-encoded body!)
````

whereas calling:
```python
request.data                   # reads from the stream, parses the form data, caches the form data
                                       # and caches the remaining body (which is now empty, and will return `""`)
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
request.form                   # returns the cached form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
```

see here for a detailed explanation: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/bb21bf90b0b121e3ed45b9950b823e4b43a81fd8/src/werkzeug/wrappers/request.py#L385-L390

so that the parser will potentially behave differently depending on previous calls to `request.data` or `request.get_data(parse_form_data=True)`.
i'm not sure exactly how to address this.

after writing all this and thinking a bit more about the werkzeug API, i think it may be the best call to NOT call `get_data` at all, and just rely on the properties `request.data`, `request.values`, `request.form`, `request.args`, etc, which seems to be the way it was intended.
you lose the bit of convenience that `as_text=True` provides, but at then the behavior will be consistent.

I was playing with this branch locally, there's one little "inconsistency", but I just discovered that it's actually fine.

---

A test is worth a thousand words:

```Python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, TypeAdapter
from pydantic.fields import FieldInfo
from pydantic.json_schema import GenerateJsonSchema
from typing_extensions import Annotated


class SubItem(BaseModel):
    name: str


class Item(BaseModel):
    sub: Annotated[SubItem, Field(description="sub item", examples=[{"name": "foo"}])]


def test_model_json_schema():
    # insert_assert(Item.model_json_schema())
    assert Item.model_json_schema() == {
        "$defs": {
            "SubItem": {
                "properties": {"name": {"title": "Name", "type": "string"}},
                "required": ["name"],
                "title": "SubItem",
                "type": "object",
            }
        },
        "properties": {
            "sub": {
                "allOf": [{"$ref": "#/$defs/SubItem"}],
                "description": "sub item",
                "examples": [{"name": "foo"}],
            }
        },
        "required": ["sub"],
        "title": "Item",
        "type": "object",
    }


def test_build_schema_for_fieldinfo():
    field_info = FieldInfo.from_annotation(
        Annotated[SubItem, Field(description="sub item", examples=[{"name": "foo"}])]
    )

    model_field_schema = TypeAdapter.model_field_schema("sub", field_info)
    # insert_assert(GenerateJsonSchema().generate_inner(model_field_schema))
    assert GenerateJsonSchema().generate_inner(model_field_schema) == {
        "$ref": "#/$defs/SubItem",
        "description": "sub item",
        "examples": [{"name": "foo"}],
    }
```

(this test passes, but has a tiny inconsistency in the layout of the data)

I'm using `generator.generate_inner()` becasue that's what I actually use (and need) in FastAPI, to be able to get schemas that use `$ref` and that are part of a bigger schema (OpenAPI, including all the JSON Schemas, to be referenced with `$ref`).

The little inconsistency is that in an equivalent Pydantic model, the field ends up with:

```
"allOf": [{"$ref": "#/$defs/SubItem"}]
```

I think this is because a previous version of JSON Schema didn't allow `$ref` to have sibling keys.

but in the `TyperAdapter` version it ends up with:

```
"$ref": "#/$defs/SubItem"
```

Nevertheless, the latest JSON Schema supports having `$ref` with siblings, so this is actually correct: https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/json-schema-core.html#appendix-G-2.6.1.10

And it renders fine in Swagger UI with OpenAPI. So this would definitely work for me!

Just wanted to mention the inconsistency I noticed between generating JSON Schema from a model and from a type adapter, but it's not something that affects me, just in case it points to something incorrect internally in some way.

I'm fine with the added functionality, but I want to note the following caveats:

- Users can still get `MemcachedUnexpectedCloseError`, even with this change. This is a distributed system, so it will be very easy for the timing to work out such that the connection looks "live" on the client side, but has become "idle" on the server side.
- Users still need to expect and handle `MemcacheUnexpectedCloseError`. I'm starting to think, after recent discussions, that we need to provide more guidance for how to do this. There is no way to guarantee that you will never get this exception.

With those caveats, I think this can eliminate some spurious cases of `MemcacheUnexpectedCloseError`, which should make normal error handling a little more stream-lined.

One minor note that doesn't matter here, but will for the next PR: you should usually make a "topic branch" to work on a PR like this, so that your `main` branch can be updated to new versions of the shared `main` without losing your changes.  The [GitHub Flow](https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/) doc has more information on that.  Once this is merged, you'll want to do something like

```
git checkout main
git fetch https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster main
git reset --hard FETCH_HEAD
```
and that will reset your `main` branch to the same commit as the taskcluster repo's `main` branch. Then you can create a topic branch for your next project:
```
git checkout -b my-next-project
# (or whatever you want to call the branch)
```

I thought a little more about this, and I don't think that `send_to_all` is the best approach.

If you're using `HashClient`, the expectation for all of the other commands is that you'll be routed to a single ("random") host. The `stats` command isn't available via `HashClient` for this reason.

I think the `send_to_all` option should be removed, and if the primary use case for `raw_command` is to target a specific host (or all hosts), then `HashClient` shouldn't have `raw_command`.

Separately, we could consider whether a "send to all" or "broadcast" system makes sense for `HashClient` (or any pool of clients).

Across the commented spots, besides minor issues and the existing suggestions, I see two major issues to be discussed more broadly:
- `.url` vs `.local_filename` in the presence of the METS server: To me it seems there still has been a confusion here. Whether I use a METS server should not relate at all to whether a referenced file is a URL or local path (i.e. whether files are downloaded from their original/source location). So the METS server should usually pass on both of these attributes. And if indeed we want to use the METS server itself to also handle files remotely (see next point), as is currently implemented with `add_file(content=...)`, then IIUC that **must not** be referenced remotely (as `url`) but only locally (as `local_filename`).
- `content` kwarg and "remote workspaces": I don't see the point of squeezing the file content through the METS server at all. The METS server must be where the METS file is. (And so must the processor for various reasons.) We have the `content` kwarg of `Workspace.add_file` as a convenience for serialization to METS and to filesystem in one shot. If that METS is behind a server, you might argue, the fs action must coincide exactly with the METS action. But temporary inconsistency is unavoidable anyway – and for two reasons: a) because there would be no guarantee when the server synchronises the METS to the fs, and b) because  `add_file` can be used with existing files (`content=None`). The task of the METS server is to synchronise METS actions, not fs actions. So let's keep the `content` kwarg out of the client-server logic.

> I was wondering whether we should decouple this further, so that a processor can request a file and download it via the METS server, store it in a local scratch directory (or just keep in memory if feasible), do the processing and upload the file again. That way there would be no need to have a processor-local full workspace at all.

The current METS server only has facilities for _output_ (`add_file(content=...)`), but not for _input_ (`download_file(...)`). Assuming that we can rewrite / guarantee all processors to do all I/O via the (extended) METS server, then processors would not need to run in the workspace directory, yes. And the client side could keep files in memory by some criterion (albeit not for bashlib processors of course, and with significant extra effort to avoid leaks and invalidity), yes. 

But what would we need that for? Processors need fast I/O. Having the data near the CPU (in memory or RAM disks or other fast storage) is one side, the other is latency of the transfer and whether that transfer contributes to reduced throughput because it cannot be adequately multiplexed with processing. So we must look at the overall architecture (esp. of parallelisation options).

For the moment we already have `Workspace.download_file` (which is used almost 100% for input retrieval in the current processor codebase). If the file is a URL, this will silently (without modifying the METS) download it to a reproducible temporary location. Subsequent calls will reuse the same file (i.e. avoid redownload). So if we avoid paying the cost of the initial transfer, and just keep URLs for all images of the workspace, we can currently get a system without much initial latency (processing will start after downloading the first page's input file). As soon as we move towards page-parallel processing, we will also have  good utilisation of CPU resources (where some workers process while others transfer; the number of jobs should be a little higher than the number of CPU cores available).

The alternative would be an explicit transfer model: prior download to a fast location, explicit queueing of transfer jobs intertwined by processing jobs. But we are still far away from such an architecture IMO. (And I don't quite see the METS I/O server fit into that paradigm anyway.)

> Maybe we should first try to identify a spot where the METS Server fits the best in this [architecture](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OCR-D/spec/c09bf8f6792af7d8a920a58700d784d02d146643/images/web-api-distributed-queue.jpg). I think the METS Server fits the best between the Processing Servers (workers) and the Network File System. It acts as a proxy when a path to the image has to be provided or an image to be downloaded. So the Mets server will not have to store workspace images locally. Since I did not put much thought into this idea yet, maybe there are things I have not considered.

I disagree. In the original conception, the METS server had nothing to do with I/O of files other than METS. For the moment, the `content` handling is partial (only output) and not necessary. I believe it is an internal detail of the Processing Servers (→Workers) which allows a multitude of threads/processes working on the same workspace. Of course, we could manage central METS servers from the Processing Broker (→Processing Server), too. 

BTW, with the page-parallel processor API, if we factor METS actions out of `process_page` (making it a function returning OcrdFile instead of a procedure returning boolean), we could even write the new central loop `process_workspace` using multiprocessing with METS synchronisation without METS server.

Hey @rjmcginness, i think we need one more round of polishing and then we're good to go.

I took the liberty of correcting the integration tests (which had several problems) and adding the corresponding snapshots.
Just to clarify:
* We typically run newly added integration tests locally first to make sure they don't have any major issues
* The `@pytest.mark.aws_validated` marker indicates that someone has validated this test against AWS, meaning the test was executed against the real remote AWS with an AWS account and the test has passed. This can be done by setting `TEST_TARGET=AWS_CLOUD` and running the tests
* snapshot tests are a bit tricky (which is why i thought i would add them to make things easier), but in combination with `TEST_TARGET=AWS_CLOUD`, you can set `SNAPSHOT_UPDATE=1`, which will execute the test against AWS, and also record the response (see the updated snapshot json file), when you remove `SNAPSHOT_UPDATE=1` again, the test will verify against the recorded AWS response

What's left to do is to fix the error responses according to the snapshots :-) We should see the test results from our CI in about 30 minutes.
The Web API would also have to be tested for exposing such types.

Overall looks good, thanks @oke-aditya. I have a few comments inline. 

Apart from them, there is the larger issue of annotating inputs with `nn.Module` rather than a more precise type. In general, we should make the input types as lenient and the output types as strict as possible. Meaning if an `nn.Module` has all teh interfaces we need within our implementation, we shouldn't force a specific type as input. On the other hand, if we return two possible subtypes of `nn.Module`, it would be better to return `Union[Foo, Bar]` instead of `nn.Module`. The former case carries more information for static type checkers to work with.

Note that we need to relax that general rule a bit, since we operate on `torch.jit > mypy`. For example, in some cases `Sequence` would be a much better annotation than `List` or `Tuple`, but since `torch.jit` does not (yet?) support `Sequence` we can't use it. You can run our test suite to see if there are any issues with this.

Thanks for the PR; sorry for the long wait!
While this is a useful QoL improvement for some users, I am worried that this change would promote unhealthy design choices. The strength of GraphQL is its typed schema. Creating a mutation that implicitly requires different combinations of fields may lead to user confusion.

We should definitely pick this up since the `@oneOf` feature will probably be added to spec, as that would resolve the issues of implicitness: https://github.com/graphql/graphql-spec/pull/825

However, this PR wouldn't support all use cases of `@oneOf`, e.G. nested `@oneOf`s inside of input types. I think the logic for this would better fit into the `Argument` handling.

If someone can point out additional examples of why this PR is still helpful, I am happy to reconsider. In the meantime, I think the technical implementation of this feature needs some extra discussion.

@sarahsonder you're definitely on the right track! Here are a few responses to your comments/questions:

1. For loop targets can also be subscript/attribute nodes, basically anything that can be on the left-hand side of a `=` in an assignment statement. This is bad practice, but possible.
2. However, I realized that you can also have nested loop targets, e.g. `for (..., ...), ... in ...`. So rather than enumerating all possible types of loop target, why don't you instead use the `nodes_of_class` recursive method, which will let you iterate over all `AssignName` nodes in the target, regardless of which type it is.
3. Regarding your test cases, my advice is to save your test code in files and try running PythonTA on each file. This way you can see whether the error is being detected, or not detected, successfully in each case.

Thanks, Vlad, for this significant contribution!  Embracing the cuDNN v8 API, as this PR does for the Convolution and Deconvolution operator, is an important step forward for MXNet.

The v8 API treats operations generically, where the same function calls are used for creating, plan-finalizing, and executing ops.  This is inherently more scalable and supportable as the library of ops grows.  Also with the cuDNN v8 API comes a more powerful representation of convolution implementations- rather than being one of a handful of choices identified by an "algo number", the v8 API expands this notion to "engine configs" (a combination of primary engine number and lesser parameters known as "knobs").  This enhanced approach should result in increased performance, particularly for non-classic models.

Users should be aware that with the move to the cuDNN v8 API, the actual conv kernels used may change, although this has always been true of even minor release updates.  In case of an undesirable change in model behavior, this PR provides the hooks (via environment variables) for troubleshooting and filtering out certain conv engines (based on numerical notes or engine id).

And finally, I'm excited that with this PR MXNet now 'owns' the convolution autotuning process.  That should allow future PRs to improve on the determinism of the autotuning result.  This PR introduces an autotuner that evaluates a conv engine config by the average of 3 timings.  Should this introduce an unwanted delay in model startup, the user can turn off autotuning with MXNET_CUDNN_AUTOTUNE_DEFAULT=0.  The convolution engine configs would then be chosen via heuristics, which have been improved considerably in the cuDNN v8 release.

Again, in summary, LGTM.
